text: From the paper "A Systematic Approach Towards Web Preservation" (2019) by Arif Ur Rahman: tractor (DNSE) is a tool developed to facilitate the extraction of news stories from the online newspapers and to migrate to a normalized format for preservation. The normalized format also includes a step to add metadata in the Digital News Stories Archive (DNSA) for future use.56 To facilitate the accessibility of news articles preserved from multiple sources, some mechanisms need to be adopted for linking the archived digital news articles. An effective term-based approach “Common Ratio Measure for Stories (CRMS)” for linking digital news articles in DNSA is introduced that links similar news articles during the preservation process.57 The approach is empirically analyzed, and the results of the proposed approach are compared to get conclusive arguments. The initial results computed automatically using a common ratio measure for stories are encouraging and are compared with the simila
label: Paper: A Systematic Approach Towards Web Preservation (2019)

text: From the paper "A Systematic Approach Towards Web Preservation" (2019) by Arif Ur Rahman: lusive arguments. The initial results computed automatically using a common ratio measure for stories are encouraging and are compared with the similarity of news articles based on human judgment. The results are generalized by defining a threshold value based on multiple experimental results using the proposed approach. Currently, there is ongoing work to extend the scope of DNSA to dual languages, i.e., Urdu and English, as well as content-based similarity measures to link news articles published in Urdu-English. Moreover, research is underway to develop tools for exploiting the linkage created among stories during the preservation process for search and retrieval tasks. SUMMARY Effective strategic planning is critical in creating web archives; hence, it requires a well-understood and a well-planned preservation process. The process should result in a well-organized web archive that in
label: Paper: A Systematic Approach Towards Web Preservation (2019)

text: From the paper "A Systematic Approach Towards Web Preservation" (2019) by Arif Ur Rahman: hives; hence, it requires a well-understood and a well-planned preservation process. The process should result in a well-organized web archive that includes not only the content to be preserved but also the contextual information required to interpret the content. The study attempts to answer many questions by guiding the archivists and related personnel, such as: How to lead the web preservation process effectively? How to initiate the preservation process? How to proceed through different steps? What are the possible techniques that may help to create a well-organized web archive? How can the archived information can be used to its greatest potential? To answer these questions, the study resulted in an appropriate step-by-step process for web preservation and a well-organized web archive. text: From the paper "Rotation invariant person tracker using top view" (2019) by Arif Ur Rahman: Vol.:(0123456789)1 3 Journal of Ambient Intelligence and Humanized Computing (2023) 14:15343–15359 https://doi.org/10.1007/s12652-019-01526-5 ORIGINAL RESEARCH Rotation invariant person tracker using top view Kaleem Ullah1 · Imran Ahmed1 · Misbah Ahmad1 · Arif Ur Rahman2 · Muhammad Nawaz1 · Awais Adnan1 Received: 31 May 2019 / Accepted: 26 September 2019 / Published online: 4 October 2019 © Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany, part of Springer Nature 2019 Abstract Person tracking is considered an important application in the field of video surveillance. A top view camera provides a wide coverage of scene and better handling of occlusion as compared to frontal view. The proposed top view based person tracking method mainly contains four modules, namely BLOB detection, standardisation, size estimation and tracking. In blob detection, foreground is extracted using segmentation, statistical operati
label: Paper: Rotation invariant person tracker using top view (2019)

text: From the paper "Rotation invariant person tracker using top view" (2019) by Arif Ur Rahman: mely BLOB detection, standardisation, size estimation and tracking. In blob detection, foreground is extracted using segmentation, statistical operation, connected component labelling and morphological operations. The top view is radial symmetric thus using this property, the extracted blob is transformed to upright position using geometric transformations. This effectively makes the blob/person rotation invariant in the scene. Basic shape based features including width, height and body ratio are measured for each blob. On the basis of these features, the algorithm effectively distinguishes a person, no person as well as merged person blob. A simple tracker for each blob is formerly created to maintain different param- eters for example, blob name, blob width & height, blob x & y coordinates, blob time, blob history, blob size and blob ratio. The proposed algorithm along with the seven a
label: Paper: Rotation invariant person tracker using top view (2019)

text: From the paper "Rotation invariant person tracker using top view" (2019) by Arif Ur Rahman: ob name, blob width & height, blob x & y coordinates, blob time, blob history, blob size and blob ratio. The proposed algorithm along with the seven attractive/popular tracking methods have been tested on different test sequences. The experimental results show that the proposed algorithm significantly improves results by achieving true detection rate of 95%. Keywords Video surveillance · Computer vision · Person tracking · Geometric transformation · Connected component labelling 1 Introduction The problem of person tracking is one of the active, inter - esting and challenging topics in the domain of computer vision. It is because of its wide range applications in modern surveillance systems. The applications include recognition of suspicious activi- ties and behaviours in shopping malls, airports, educational institutions and hospitals Tseng et al. (2014) and Migniot and Ababsa (2016). M
label: Paper: Rotation invariant person tracker using top view (2019)

(2014) and Migniot and Ababsa (2016). Moreover, counting of persons is another important application and researchers have explored it (Hao et al. 2018; Fernando et al. 2018; Dadi et al. 2017). In addition to this, person tracking can be done in specific environments like the industrial sector Ahmed et al. (2018). Furthermore, approaches have been developed for full body pose tracking and gait recognition Sigalas et al. (2016) and Shu et al. (2015). Although, the growth of computer technol- ogy results in creating new challenges but at the same time it is creating opportunities for researchers. In the context of person tracking, a lot of techniques have been developed by researchers Ahmad et al. (2019b). Some examples of devel- oped frontal view person tracking systems are highli

text: From the paper "Rotation invariant person tracker using top view" (2019) by Arif Ur Rahman: ot of techniques have been developed by researchers Ahmad et al. (2019b). Some examples of devel- oped frontal view person tracking systems are highlighted in Fig. 1. Person tracking becomes difficult because of occlu- sion in most of the cases. For example, it can be seen in Fig. 1 that the person is occluded with another person or other object which results in false detection. One of the effective solutions to handle this problem is to use the top view camera as shown in Fig. 2. text: From the paper "Rotation invariant person tracker using top view" (2019) by Arif Ur Rahman: 4872@gmail.com Muhammad Nawaz muhammad.nawaz@imsciences.edu.pk Awais Adnan awais.adnan@imsciences.edu.pk 1 Centre of Excellence in IT, Institute of Management Sciences, Peshawar, Pakistan 2 Department of Computer Science, Bahria University, Islamabad, Pakistan 15344 K. Ullah et al. 1 3 (2019b). These top view based methods are effective but suffer from failure when considering a wide scene. It can be seen in Fig. 2 that person tracking has been done by consid- ering narrow field of view. One of the reason that research- ers use narrow field of view might be use of the circular or square region of interest i.e just below the camera (Head or Head-shoulder). On the other hand, the use of wide field of view provides more coverage of the scene at the cost of different oriented poses and size variation (see Fig. 6a).The pose and size of the person vary with respect to radial dis- tance. This i
label: Paper: Rotation invariant person tracker using top view (2019)

text: From the paper "Rotation invariant person tracker using top view" (2019) by Arif Ur Rahman: t the cost of different oriented poses and size variation (see Fig. 6a).The pose and size of the person vary with respect to radial dis- tance. This is one of the major issues faced by researchers when using top view. However, the top view person detec- tion method can be more effective and informative by using wide coverage area. In this work, an effective algorithm is proposed which uses geometric transformation to make a person in the upright pose which makes person pose rotation invariant i.e Figs. 6a and 7d. The four-persons having completely dif- ferent poses and orientations will look similar in an upright pose. In this paper, a top view blob based person tracking algorithm is proposed which helps to overcome the above mentioned issue and also helps in reducing camera installa- tion cost, camera energy consumption, human resource and occlusion handling as identified by Ahmad et al
label: Paper: Rotation invariant person tracker using top view (2019)

text: From the paper "Rotation invariant person tracker using top view" (2019) by Arif Ur Rahman: e and also helps in reducing camera installa- tion cost, camera energy consumption, human resource and occlusion handling as identified by Ahmad et al. (2019a). The proposed algorithm consists of pre-processing steps using geometric transformation to standardise the blob fol- lowed by shape-based features to estimate single size of the person. The main contribution of this paper is as follows: – Recording of new top view dataset in order to track a person in wide coverage area having multiple sizes, poses and orientations. – Overhead view is circular symmetric and thus the pro - posed algorithm exhibits property of rotation invariance while tracking persons. – A blob-based method along with geometric transforma- tion is developed that transforms all oblique shape blobs to standard upright positions for easy estimation of dif- ferent parameters of person blob. – Person blob standardisatio
label: Paper: Rotation invariant person tracker using top view (2019)

text: From the paper "Rotation invariant person tracker using top view" (2019) by Arif Ur Rahman: forms all oblique shape blobs to standard upright positions for easy estimation of dif- ferent parameters of person blob. – Person blob standardisation is achieved which not only helps in decision making of merging and splitting, but gives better estimation of distinguishing person from no person. From the above discussion it is concluded that mostly researcher uses narrow field of view considering region of interest as head and head shoulder, furthermore extending narrow field of view to wide coverage area causes signif- icantly change in pose, size and mainly in orientation of person which are not addressed in literature. Therefore, the main objective of proposed algorithm is to developed such algorithm which takes these parameters into account while tracking person and easily cope with these variations. 3 Dataset In frontal view datasets, the problem of occlusion can- not be handled. On the other hand in available top view datasets, the field of view is narrow, number of subjects are small considering directly below the camera and recorded in constrained illumination and li
label: Paper: Rotation invariant person tracker using top view (2019)

text: From the paper "Rotation invariant person tracker using top view" (2019) by Arif Ur Rahman: asets, the field of view is narrow, number of subjects are small considering directly below the camera and recorded in constrained illumination and lightning con- ditions. Keeping these things in consideration, the new top view dataset named IMS-Person Tracking IMS-PT is recorded using Point Grey Blackfly camera with Fujinon Wide Angle lens. The camera is installed at the main entrance of the Institute of Management Sciences, Peshawar, Pakistan at a height of about 7 meters from the ground. Due to its wide angle lens, the camera covers a wider coverage area and providing more visibility of the scene. The recorded dataset, contains a total of 30 videos with of resolution of 640 × 480 pixels. The video is recorded at 20 frames per second in MPEG format with the compression rate of 75%. The average size of the person image is almost 132 kb. Data is recorded under normal daylight conditions
label: Paper: Rotation invariant person tracker using top view (2019)

text: From the paper "Rotation invariant person tracker using top view" (2019) by Arif Ur Rahman: PEG format with the compression rate of 75%. The average size of the person image is almost 132 kb. Data is recorded under normal daylight conditions at dif- ferent hours during daytime. The recorded dataset contains both male and female subjects with different age, height, weight and colour of clothes. The developed dataset is presented in Fig. 3. 4 Rotation invariant person tracker The simple and effective top view blob based person track - ing algorithm is briefly discussed in this section. The algo- rithm is mainly divided into two modules: in the first mod- ule, segmentation along with some geometric transformation is used for extraction of person blob while in the second, tracker list is created which enables the developed tracker to track the desired person blob in frames. The simplicity of developed person tracking algorithm improves computa- tion time. The details explanation of

When a new person blob is received, the tracker object/list is created containing blob information. The tracker object is extended by updating the new blob information for each frame. If the blob is received followed by new informa- tion, the new information is compared with already existing one. If new information significantl

If the blob is received followed by new informa- tion, the new information is compared with already existing one. If new information significantly overlaps/match with existing, it is ignored and tracking is sustained. Otherwise, a new tracker object is created for new detected blob. A tracker is discontinued if the tracking algorithm fails to detect information for the next few frames. For each blob, the velocity and speed is estimated. Using the blob informa- tion, the tracking direction has also been found using angle /u1D703 information. The blob position history is stored in case the blob is lost for next few frames. Ullah et al. 1 3 5 Experiments and results The developed system was tested on a set of video sequences. Due to space limitation the result of four sequences are pre- sented in this section. The video sequences contain a person at different locations within the wide scene. For evaluation purposes, original tracking trajectories vs detected, disparity graph, actual detected positions of person have been dis- cussed. Ground truth is used to check the performance of proposed method. The ground truth
label: Paper: Rotation invariant person tracker using top view (2019)

Ground truth is used to check the performance of proposed method. The ground truth, for each video sequence is labeled manually. Furthermore the ROC curve for TDR and FDR of the proposes method is presented. The experi- mental results of the proposed method are presented in the sequel. 5.1 Detected tracking trajectories vs ground truth The results of detected tracking trajectories against label ground truth trajectories are shown in Fig. 13. It can be clearly see in the Fig. 13, that the result of detected tracking trajectories is almost near to original labeled ground truth. For each video sequence in the Fig. 13, the detected tracking trajectories are represented by red lines and green lines show the original ground truth trajectories of video sequences. The person movement is plotted against frame num- ber as shown in below Fig. 14. The frame number, x posi- tions and y position of the person have been shown on 3D graph using x axis, y axis and z axis respectively. 5.2 Performance evaluation blob based person tracking method The performance of the proposed method, is evaluated using disparity graphs. The disparity is actually calculated by using the difference between the actual and calculated positions. It is the distance between the actual and detected centroid positions of the blob. The disparity graphs shows, how the proposed method correctly detects the person blob fo
label: Paper: Rotation invariant person tracker using top view (2019)

The disparity graphs shows, how the proposed method correctly detects the person blob for each video sequence. The actual centroid positions for the person is obtained from ground truth. For each video sequences the disparity graph is shown in Fig. 15. For each of the video sequence, the true detection rate is calculated using different threshold radii R as shown in Table 4. In the table, it can be seen that at threshold 20, good results have been achieved for each of the video sequence. In Table 4 the true detection rate is shown against radius thresholds. At threshold 20, the proposed algorithm achieved accuracy of 95%. The ROC curve for True detection rate can be seen in the Fig. 16. The average FPR for the proposed algorithm is 5% as shown in Fig. 17. 5.3 Comparison with state of art tracking algorithms In computer vision, person tracking is a very common problem. Mostly C++ programming language and Open- CV libraries are used for implementation of tracking algo- rithms. In this work, seven different tracking algorithms are compared on newly recorded top view dataset. These track- ing algorithms are MIL (Babenko et al. 2009), BOOSTING Grabner et al. ( 2006), KCF Henriques et al. ( 2012), TLD Kalal et al. (2012), MEDIANFLOW Kalal et al. (2010 ), MOSSE Bolme et al. ( 2010) and CSRT Lukezic et al. (2017). These trackers are designed to track the person from the frontal view. The predefined
label: Paper: Rotation invariant person tracker using top view (2019)

text: From the paper "Rotation invariant person tracker using top view" (2019) by Arif Ur Rahman: 0 ), MOSSE Bolme et al. ( 2010) and CSRT Lukezic et al. (2017). These trackers are designed to track the person from the frontal view. The predefined bounding box is used to create the trackers for overhead view. The whole body of the person is tracked and in the second; only the head of the person is tracked. The results of both cases are shown in Sect. 5 . The results of each algorithm vary according to the situation. The variation in the tracker results are due to the camera position, person pose and changes in the direction of person body or head. In this research work, the focus is on the positions that are tracked in most frames and considered best. The results show that most of the trackers that give best results in other scenarios perform poorly when overhead view is considered. All these algorithms are currently avail- able in OpenCV (Table 6). The performance measure of differe
label: Paper: Rotation invariant person tracker using top view (2019)

text: From the paper "Rotation invariant person tracker using top view" (2019) by Arif Ur Rahman: erform poorly when overhead view is considered. All these algorithms are currently avail- able in OpenCV (Table 6). The performance measure of different tracking algorithms are shown in Fig. 18, which it is clearly seen that none of the algorithm perform well. The worst results are reported by TLD which gave almost 12% accuracy. The Median Flow algorithm also shows better results as compared to TLD but when there is a sudden change in the direction, the track - ing bounding box fails to correctly track the person head in complex positions and give a result of 42%. The BOOSTING and CSRT also report bad results and gives an accuracy of 15355Rotation invariant person tracker using top view 1 3 40%. Therefore, the head-based person tracking is considered in the second scenario. While tracking a person head the accuracy of BOOSTING and CSRT gave good results. The accuracy of the CSRT algorithm gives high accuracy of 85% as shown in Fig. 18. The CSRT helps in localization and enlarging the selected region. This property of CSRT improves tracking of non-rec- tangular objects i.e persons head. On other hand BOOSTING is decade old algorithm but works and gives accuracy of 83%. To standard- ize the person blob, geometric transformation is used. The width to height ratio for the person has been calcu- lated to decide whether the blob is person blob, multiple person blob or no person blob. For each
label: Paper: Rotation invariant person tracker using top view (2019)

text: From the paper "Habitat mapping using deep neural networks" (2020) by Arif Ur Rahman: Vol.:(0123456789)1 3 Multimedia Systems (2021) 27:679–690 https://doi.org/10.1007/s00530-020-00695-0 SPECIAL ISSUE PAPER Habitat mapping using deep neural networks Muhammad Yasir1 · Arif Ur Rahman1 · Moneeb Gohar1 Published online: 3 October 2020 © Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany, part of Springer Nature 2020 Abstract Habitat mapping is an important and challenging task that helps in monitoring, managing, and preserving ecosystems. It becomes more challenging when marine habitats are mapped, as it is difficult to get quality images in an underwater envi- ronment. Moreover, achieving good location accuracy in underwater environments is an additional issue. Sonar imagery has good quality but is hard to be analyzed. Therefore, camera imagery is used for research purposes. Our research targets marine habitats - more specifically, coral reef marine habitats. Recognition of coral reef in underwat
label: Paper: Habitat mapping using deep neural networks (2020)

text: From the paper "Habitat mapping using deep neural networks" (2020) by Arif Ur Rahman: sed for research purposes. Our research targets marine habitats - more specifically, coral reef marine habitats. Recognition of coral reef in underwater images poses a sig- nificant difficulty due to the nature of the data. Many species of coral reef have similar characteristics, i.e. higher inter-class similarity and lower intra-class similarity. Spatial borders between coral reef classes are hard to separate, as they tend to appear together in groups. For these reasons, the classification of coral reef species requires aid from marine biologists. This research work presents a technique for accurate coral reef classification using deep convolutional neural networks. The proposed approach has been validated on Moorea Labeled Corals (MLC), an imbalanced dataset, which is a subset of Moorea Coral Reef Long Term Ecological Research (MCR LTER) packaged for computer vision research. A custom
label: Paper: Habitat mapping using deep neural networks (2020)

text: From the paper "Habitat mapping using deep neural networks" (2020) by Arif Ur Rahman: n imbalanced dataset, which is a subset of Moorea Coral Reef Long Term Ecological Research (MCR LTER) packaged for computer vision research. A custom valid patch dataset is extracted using the annotation files provided with the dataset. Two image enhancement algorithms and data- driven feature extraction techniques are employed using several pre-trained deep convolutional neural networks as feature extractors. Local-SPP technique is combined with feature extractors and followed by 2-layers multi-layer perceptron (MLP) classifier to achieve high classification rates. Habitat mapping shows the geographic dis- tribution of different habitats within a particular area. Various types of images can be used for mapping habitats. In the early 2000s, major advancements were made that changed the acquisition of underwater imagery. One advancement was the widespread availability of digital cam- eras. In 2000, a scuba diver was limited to few photographs per dive, but in the 2010s, the same diver could take thou - sands of photographs per dive. The second advancement was in platforms, such as autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs), remotely operated vehicles (ROVs), and towed cameras. These platforms allow imagery in depths and with a finer spatial resolution that was previously not possible with divers. There are pros and cons of every advancement and the major advancements in ocean science are no excep- tion. Software packages are available to transfer extracted information from images to analysis; however, humans are still part of the process [3]. An automation process for the classification of under - water imagery would enhance the study of marine habitats (coral reef in this case) by reducing the analysis bottleneck of marine biologists and allowing researchers to take full advantage of underwater image datasets. A lot of efforts have been made to automate the process of classifying marine habitats, but no consensus on one single robust algorithm exists among researchers [3 ]. Mapping habitats in big regions can be a tedious and time-consuming task as regions can span wide all-through- out land or marine regions. Mapping a marine habitat becomes further difficult because the light cannot travel through seabed floors and aerial photography is not feasible as the seabed is not visible from air except in crystal clear waters. In recent years, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have superseded the handcrafted features in classification tasks [8 ]. CNNs have achieved high accuracy on classification tasks such as in ImageNet [9 ]. A summary of the existing algorithms and approaches for marine habitats is presented in Table 2. 2.3 Image enhancement techniques Image enhancement techniques are used when images are taken in an underwater environment. It is used as an important pre-processing step in research where the quality of image matters like in medical image analysis [28– 30]. Similarly, the underwater environment in which images are taken has a lot of suspended particles that cause haziness on the caught picture called turbidity. Light rays encoun- ter these particles being both scattered and absorbed. This phenomenon reduces the quality of images and hence this affects the classification accuracy [5 ]. Therefore,
label: Paper: Habitat mapping using deep neural networks (2020)

text: From the paper "Habitat mapping using deep neural networks" (2020) by Arif Ur Rahman: being both scattered and absorbed. This phenomenon reduces the quality of images and hence this affects the classification accuracy [5 ]. Therefore, image enhancement techniques play an important role in the clas- sification of underwater images. However, resulting image after enhancement is subjective to an observer, and because capturing environments are different, one specific enhance- ment technique is not there to generalize on all datasets. Cur- rent research is focusing on applying enhancements based on the characteristics of datasets [31]. Histogram equalization is used in current trends for marine image enhancement [32]. However, these techniques introduce over enhancement where the region has more fre- quent gray levels and under enhancement where there is less occurrence of gray levels. Brightness preserving dynamic histogram equalization (BPDHE) can produce a mean histo- gra
label: Paper: Habitat mapping using deep neural networks (2020)

text: From the paper "Habitat mapping using deep neural networks" (2020) by Arif Ur Rahman: enhancement where there is less occurrence of gray levels. Brightness preserving dynamic histogram equalization (BPDHE) can produce a mean histo- gram of the image where the mean intensity of output image is the same as the input image and hence overcome the limi- tations of histogram equalization [33]. Adaptive histogram equalization (AHE) differs from ordinary histogram techniques because it is a local contrast enhancement technique in which many histograms are com- puted where each histogram corresponds to a different sec- tion of a given image. In this technique, a small window or kernel slides over the pixels of the image sequentially and enhances those pixels that fall under it. More details and local information are observed. However, the cost of sliding the overlapped sub-blocks is high and over-amplification of noise is likely [34]. Contrast limited adaptive histogram equalizat
label: Paper: Habitat mapping using deep neural networks (2020)

text: From the paper "Habitat mapping using deep neural networks" (2020) by Arif Ur Rahman: r, the cost of sliding the overlapped sub-blocks is high and over-amplification of noise is likely [34]. Contrast limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE) removes the over-amplification of the noise effect of AHE. In this technique, the given image is divided into non-overlapping regions of equal sizes, and histograms are computed for each of them. Histograms obtained are clipped based on desired contrast expansion and grayscale mapping is performed by obtaining a transformation function. Region boundaries are smoothed by bi-linear interpolation at the end. However, it produces ring artifacts at strong edges and over-amplification of noise at flat regions [35]. In a study different algorithms on the 3D TURBID dataset for image enhancement and restoration have been evaluated [5]. They concluded that contrast limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE) outperforms all the state-
label: Paper: Habitat mapping using deep neural networks (2020)

text: From the paper "Habitat mapping using deep neural networks" (2020) by Arif Ur Rahman: ement and restoration have been evaluated [5]. They concluded that contrast limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE) outperforms all the state-of-the-art algorithms. There are other approaches which focused on the contrast limited adaptive histogram specification (CLAHE), Color channel stretching, normalization, color correction, and gamma correction [19, 31, 36]. The approaches devel- oped to involve the use of HSV, LAB, RGB color spaces with coral images for enhancements such as color channel and intensity stretching [4]. It contains a total of 2055 images from the years 2008, 2009, and 2010. 2008 has 689 images, 2009 has 695, and 2010 has 689 images. It contains ( ∼ 30 categories) according to an established taxonomy for the site. However, the authors have used
label: Paper: Habitat mapping using deep neural networks (2020)

text: From the paper "Habitat mapping using deep neural networks" (2020) by Arif Ur Rahman: 009 has 695, and 2010 has 689 images. It contains ( ∼ 30 categories) according to an established taxonomy for the site. However, the authors have used nine most dominant classes including four non-corals, namely crustose coral - line algae (CCA), turf algae, sand and macroalgae, and five corals genera, namely Pavona, Montipora, Acropara, Pocil- lopora, and Porites. Each image has its own annotation file in which 200 randomly chosen points are annotated. Figures 1 and 2 present sample annotation file with twenty annota - tions and a sample image of the dataset superimposed by its annotations using its annotation file, respectively. 4 Methodology The architecture of the proposed approach is presented in Fig. 3. Four different patch sizes, i.e. 28, 56, 84 and 112, are extracted, centered at each data point (along with the ground truth) in the image. Patches are then preprocessed using image
label: Paper: Habitat mapping using deep neural networks (2020)

text: From the paper "Habitat mapping using deep neural networks" (2020) by Arif Ur Rahman: . 28, 56, 84 and 112, are extracted, centered at each data point (along with the ground truth) in the image. Patches are then preprocessed using image enhancement algorithms and followed by scal- ing them according to the chosen pre-trained CNN model, as different pre-trained models have different image sizes for their input. Resized patches are fed into local spatial pyramid pooling (local-SPP) for efficient representation of the neigh- bourhood of data point and to make features scale-invariant [6]. Feature vector obtained from local-SPP is then fed into a two-layer multi-layer perceptron (MLP) model to classify them into nine classes. 4.1 Patches extraction A set of random images from 2008, 2009, and 2010 are chosen along with there annotation files for patches extrac- tion. Patches are centered around the data point along with its label from the annotation file. At first, a fixed siz
label: Paper: Habitat mapping using deep neural networks (2020)

text: From the paper "Habitat mapping using deep neural networks" (2020) by Arif Ur Rahman: tation files for patches extrac- tion. Patches are centered around the data point along with its label from the annotation file. At first, a fixed size of 112 × 112 is selected for the extraction of patches but certain problems arise. A patch size of 112 × 112 might be over - whelmed by the surroundings. A small patch might not cap- ture longer textures. Histograms would be more stable if a larger area is used. When the point of interest is in between edges of two classes, any patch size will be problematic. These problems are presented in Fig. 5. The selection of a fixed-size patch is not appropriate to tackle problems illustrated in Fig. 5. Therefore, the proposed method includes a step to implement a hybrid patch size approach to evaluate the performance. An evaluation of the single patch size with and with- out image enhancement algorithms and the hybrid patch size with image enhance
label: Paper: Habitat mapping using deep neural networks (2020)

An evaluation of the single patch size with and with- out image enhancement algorithms and the hybrid patch size with image enhancement algorithm is presented in Table 3. DenseNet-169 was used as the pre-trained model and CLAHE as the image enhancement algorithm for these experiments, as their combination yielded maximum accu- racy in all experiments. The evaluation shows that the hybrid patch size approach is superior to any of the single patch size approaches. Fig. 1 Sample annotation file Fig. 2 Annotation-imposed sample image 684 M. Patch extraction of those data points that are near to the edges of the frame on all four sides is eliminated during the patch extraction process by calcu- lating pixel-width and pixel-height of edges of the frame on all four sides and discarding patches that contain calculated pixels of edges. Because patches around those points contain parts of blue frame and classifier would treat it as a feature which is not desired. After removing those frames contained patches, there arises another problem where some patches contain a white transect line and some contain orange thread. They are problematic and need to be removed when extract- ing such patches. Now there are a lot of patches since four are extracted from each data point and it is manually difficult to go through all patches and remove
label: Paper: Habitat mapping using deep neural networks (2020)

text: From the paper "Habitat mapping using deep neural networks" (2020) by Arif Ur Rahman: atches. Now there are a lot of patches since four are extracted from each data point and it is manually difficult to go through all patches and remove them. So, the problem of threads-contained patches is tackled with the K -means clustering algorithm during the patch extraction. After a 112 × 112 patch extraction, K -means is applied with K = 5 (after hit and trial) to specify the num- ber of clusters to extract using pixels intensities as input and plot extracted clusters of colors and their RGB val- ues on the color wheel. Now suppose a patch with orange thread comes, K -means algorithm runs and extracts five dominant color clusters and plot them on the color wheel with their color codes. Color code values of clusters are converted into CIELAB space and are matched one by one with input color codes of orange and yellow with a certain threshold, which specifies how different can two co
label: Paper: Habitat mapping using deep neural networks (2020)

text: From the paper "Habitat mapping using deep neural networks" (2020) by Arif Ur Rahman: CIELAB space and are matched one by one with input color codes of orange and yellow with a certain threshold, which specifies how different can two colors be. If the difference falls within the limit of threshold then the patch is discarded. Color codes of orange and yellow are given RGB color space and are converted in CIELAB space to match with extracted color codes. The threshold plays an important role. Consider a case where we are try - ing to find images with blue color. If the threshold is too high, green images are highly likely. On the other hand, If threshold is too low, then dark blue is highly likely. It’s all situation-dependent and so we need to set threshold carefully. Color difference was measured using CIE76 formula which was proposed in 1976 as first formula for color dif- ference in CIELAB coordinates [37 ]. Two of them, namely contrast limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE) and color chan- nel stretching are used in the proposed approach as shown in Fig. 3. The algorithms are used for image enhancement on the patches that were extracted from the dataset. The reason for choosing CLAHE is that it is prevalent among researchers in literature for image enhancement and color channel stretching is specifically developed for underwater images enhancement. 4.2.1 Contrast limited adaptive histogram equalization In CLAHE, an image is divided into non-overlapping regions of equal sizes and histograms are computed
label: Paper: Habitat mapping using deep neural networks (2020)

text: From the paper "Habitat mapping using deep neural networks" (2020) by Arif Ur Rahman: ontrast limited adaptive histogram equalization In CLAHE, an image is divided into non-overlapping regions of equal sizes and histograms are computed for each of them. Histograms obtained are clipped based on desired contrast expansion and grayscale mapping is performed by obtaining a transformation function. Region boundaries are smoothed by bi-linear interpolation at the end. 4.2.2 Color channel stretching In color channel stretching, 1% and 99% intensity percen- tile are found for each channel separately and then subtract- ing the lower from all intensities and divided by the upper. It also sets any negative pixel values to zero during sub- traction. It is found out to be better then intensity channel Stretch which stretches an image globally across all chan - nels, although it modifies color images slightly. 4.3 Transfer learning for habitat classification The proposed methodology ca
label: Paper: Habitat mapping using deep neural networks (2020)

text: From the paper "Habitat mapping using deep neural networks" (2020) by Arif Ur Rahman: bally across all chan - nels, although it modifies color images slightly. 4.3 Transfer learning for habitat classification The proposed methodology capitalizes on the abilities of transfer learning to classify habitats. Dagnet (cyclic in nature) models are employed and are used as feature extrac- tors in the local-SPP approach. 4.4 Patch resizing Patches are resized according to the requirement of the chosen pre-trained CNN model after the patch extraction step and choosing a pre-trained CNN model. For instance, If VGG was selected as a pre-trained CNN model then the required size for patches would be 224 × 224 . Interpolation algorithms are used for image enlargement. Interpolation takes known data points to estimate values at unknown data points. Interpolation in images works in two directions and achieves the best approximation of color and intensity values Fig. 4 Sample patch and its

In the proposed methodology, the authors implement a non-adap- tive bicubic algorithm for the resizing of patches because it has an idle trade-off between output quality and processing time and it is used as a standard in many professional image- editing softwares (including Adobe Photoshop), printer driv- ers, and in-camera interpolation. Since four different patch sizes (28, 56, 84, 112) centered around each data point are extracted, so all four sizes are resized according to the required size needed by the chosen pre-trained CNN model. 4.5 Local spatial pyramid pooling Pre-trained neural network mode

For instance, DenseNet requires the input image to be of a size 224 × 224 . The proposed approach includes a step of extract- ing square size patches which are multiples of 28, centred around one data point to make features scale-invariant. Each patch size extracts neighbourhood details of a single data point at a different scale. The patches need to be resized to meet to meet the input-size restriction of a given pre- trained model. Inspired by [6], the authors have adopted the local-SPP approach. The reason behind local-SPP adoption is due to different techniques of annotation in coral reef images, spatial pooling cannot be used. local-SPP is spatially pyramidal and is different from SPP [10] in that it does not d
label: Paper: Habitat mapping using deep neural networks (2020)

text: From the paper "Habitat mapping using deep neural networks" (2020) by Arif Ur Rahman: annotation in coral reef images, spatial pooling cannot be used. local-SPP is spatially pyramidal and is different from SPP [10] in that it does not divide the whole image into sub-images. Instead, it extracts patches at different scales centred around one data point. Features obtained from different scales are max-pooled to obtained one single feature vector representing maximum scale-invariant response [6]. Max-pooling enables us to cap- tures features that are most prominent in the neighbourhood of the data point, regardless of the scale at which they are extracted. Figure 6 shows the local-SPP technique in effect with DenseNet-169 as a feature extractor. Four patches of dif- ferent sizes i.e. 28 × , 56 × , 84 × , 112 × are extracted from a single data point and resized to 224 × 224 using bicu- bic interpolation. Resized patches are given as input to a local-SPP. Each of the four resi
label: Paper: Habitat mapping using deep neural networks (2020)

text: From the paper "Habitat mapping using deep neural networks" (2020) by Arif Ur Rahman: m a single data point and resized to 224 × 224 using bicu- bic interpolation. Resized patches are given as input to a local-SPP. Each of the four resized patches in local-SPP is passed to DenseNet for feature extraction and generate a 1664D vector which is the output of a convolutional part of DenseNet. Since there are four patches, four vec- tors are generated and then followed by max-pooling these four vectors to generate one single 1664D vector. Figure 7 shows how four 4D feature vectors are max-pooled into one 4D feature vector. The First 4D vector is generated from features of patch size 28 × . The second 4D vector is generated from the features of patch size 56 × . Third from 84 × and fourth from 112 × . Then these four are combined to give us a new 4D feature vector consisting of the max response of from each of four vectors to have a final vector independent of scaling. Fig. 6 Lo

It has one input layer, two hidden layers with ReLU as activation function and followed by an output layer with softmax as activation for classifying into nine habitat classes mentioned in Sect. 3. Accuracy is used as a measure to evaluate the results of the proposed approach. It is the ratio of number of cor - rect predictions to the total number of input samples. It is calculated from the confusion matrix which describes the complete performance of a model in the form of a matrix. Accura

The authors consid- ered 102,453 points randomly and extracted patches off these points. But these patches had frames and threads on them because of how images are taken underwater using a frame for alignment and threads for a precise location. The number of valid patches for training were 41,637 and for testing 10,410 after applying the techniques mentioned in Sect. 4.1. The proposed approach involves the extraction of patches of a single data point at various scales including 28 × , 56 × , 84 × and 112 × which are then passed into the local spatial pyramid. The MLC dataset is an imbalanced dataset and, therefore, has imbalanced labels distribution. The labels distribution of nine classes for training set is presented in Table 4 and for testing set is presented in Table 5. Crustose Coralline Algae (CCA) is the class with more labels than any other class and Acropara with the least class labels. To tackle this problem at training time where a neural network would be more confident in CCA class labels and less confident on others, we have used the oversampling technique synthetic minority oversampling technique (SMOTE) [38] to balance the data after the fea- ture extraction stage. SMOTE generates synthetic data based on the feature space similarities between minor - ity class samples. To create a synthetic sample, it finds the K-nearest neighbours (KNN) of each minority class (2)Accuracy = Total Correct Predictions Total Predictions Made. sample, randomly selects one of them, and then calculate linear interpolations to produce a new minority sample in the neighborhood. Therefore, using more data points in a balanced way we can achieve more accuracy. 6 Conclusions and future work The paper proposes an approach for marine habitat clas- sification which is an important aspect in habitats map - ping for monitoring and preservation of marine habitats. It targeted Coral Reef marine habitats. The Moorea Labeled Corals (MLC) dataset is used which is imbalanced and consists of images collected over three years between 2008 and 2010. A valid patch dataset was extracted from MLC from all three years randomly using the annotation files provided with the dataset. Two image enhancement algo- rithms, namely color channel stretching and contrast lim- ited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE) were used in the pre-processing the underwater optic patch images to handle the issues associated with the quality of images, light intensit
label: Paper: Habitat mapping using deep neural networks (2020)

text: From the paper "Habitat mapping using deep neural networks" (2020) by Arif Ur Rahman: n (CLAHE) were used in the pre-processing the underwater optic patch images to handle the issues associated with the quality of images, light intensity, and turbidity in underwater environments. Five experiments were conducted using color channel stretching and five experiments using CLAHE. The highest accuracy reported was 87.40% using CLAHE as an image enhancement algorithm and DenseNet-169 as a feature extractor. Although an effective coral reef classification tech- nique is proposed, there is room for further work. In the proposed approach, 52,047 data points are used to train a CNN. Since CNN requires a large amount of data, thereby adding more points would yield better accuracy. One direction could be to extract a balanced patch dataset and applying both image enhancement algorithms on patches one after the other followed by an SVM classifier. Another direction could be when employ - ing the local-SPP technique, different patch scales can be explored, for instance, using multiples of 14 to generate 14 × , 28 × , 42 × , 56 × patch sizes. text: From the paper "Model Migration Approach for Database Preservation" (2010) by Arif Ur Rahman: Approach Involves three major activities  Calculating the information embedded in code (functions, triggers, procedures)  Storing it in dimensional model instead of relational model  Export the information in the dimensional model to XML Relational Model  Relational Model of real size information systems - very complex and no quick access  Important knowledge from the application domain embedded in code  Better for transaction processing – not required for the preservation version Conclusions Alternative to preserving code in database preservation • Execute the code • Preserve the results in simple and easily accessible model  Before migration, decide what is to be kept for the future and what can be discarded • Similar to the evaluation, elimination and description work of archiving  Migration Process • Steps are documented • Results coming from the data in the dimensional mode

This proposed algorithm was tested using four recommended video sequences, including indoor and outdoor shoots, and were compared with five high ranked algorithms. Based on the values of standard performance measures, the proposed algorithm achieved an average of more than 12.30% higher accuracy results. The first step in this regard is to detect the moving objects; for this purpose, the basic operation needed for the separa- tion of objects is called foreground from the static informa- tion called background [4, 5]. Object detection is the starting step of any visual surveil- lance system. Real-time detection of the foreground object is an important step in many computer vision applications. The Background subtract is a common method used in fore- ground detection. It calculates the difference between the current image and background image and detects foreground by setting threshold [6]. Previous work [8 ] shows that the performance of tracking greatly depends on the accuracy of foreground detection, especially in multicamera system where the detec- tion error usually propagates to cameras. Several approaches have been proposed for foreground detection, and have been widely used in tracking and video surveillance in outdoor and indoor environments. These approaches include mod- eling the distribution of the background pixels [7, 9], detect- ing the motion part in the image sequence [10]. The main difficulties of motion detection in changeable background are summarized as follows: – Sudden illumination change in part of the image. – Reception coverage background period of time, i.e., it is usually not possible to obtain information about the background change. – Difficult to deal with the object and shadow diffusion profile. – Complex motion patterns, e.g., distinct mot

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In Sect. 2, we give an overview of foreground detection algorithms, Sect. 3 is related to the proposed work. Section 4 consists of Experiment results. Section 5 is about the Discussion, and at the end, Conclusion is described in Sect. 6. 2 Related work Much research has been done in the area of background esti- mation and background/foreground separation. The mov - ing target detection is first step used in video Surveillance system. A very commonly used approach is that of Stauffer and Grimson [6], who model each pixel of the background as a weighted sum of Gaussian random variables (a Gauss- ian mix

Other authors have modeled the background using richer information than just pixel color. A certain portion of the research on background estima- tion is concerned with shadows, which are often incorrectly identified as foreground. Single difference algorithm com- pares the pixels of the current and the previous frame pixels whose difference is significant (according to some more or less complex thresholding criterion) are considered to be a part of the background [11– 13]. label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: e sensors and classification algorithms influences the accuracy of LULC classification. This study evaluates the performance of three satellite sensors, GF-6 (GF-6), S2 (S2), and L9(L9), and three machine learning clas - sifiers, Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), in classifying LULC in Islamabad, Pakistan. The satellite data with high-to-course spatial resolution data was utilized, and a comprehensive pre-processing workflow ensured high-quality imagery. The results indicate that XGBoost, paired with GF-6, achieved the highest overall classification accuracy (94.24%) and kappa coefficient (0.9279), outperforming RF and SVM. S2 combined with XGBoost also showed superior performance (92.89%) compared to other sensor-algorithm combinations. The study reveals that high spatial resolution (GF-6) significantly improves LULC classificatio
label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: .89%) compared to other sensor-algorithm combinations. The study reveals that high spatial resolution (GF-6) significantly improves LULC classification, particularly in detecting forest and urban areas. Feature importance analysis identified GF-6 Red and NIR bands as the most significant predictors, especially for vegetation-related classes. The findings underscore the importance of selecting the appropriate sensor and classifier for specific LULC tasks, with XGBoost and high-resolution sensors like GF-6 providing the most accurate results. This study contributes to the growing body of research on LULC classification and offers valuable insights for urban planning and environmental monitoring. Land covers numerous activities like agriculture, mini

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: the surface of the land (e.g., grass, concrete, water) (Briassoulis 2020; Treitz & Rogan 2004). Land covers numerous activities like agriculture, mining, and forestry (Akram et al. 2022; Hussain et al. 2024a; Shobairi et al. 2022; Usoltsev et al. 2020, 2022). LULC is, therefore, one of the crucial aspects that are linked to the economic growth of the country and its people (Comber & Wulder, 2019; Koranteng et al. 2023; Nti Asamoah et al. 2018a; Haider et al. 2017; Jallat et al. 2021; Khan et al. 2020). LULC, when analyzed globally, significantly impacts eco- system services, biodiversity, and biogeochemical cycles (Hussain et al. 2024b). They also contribute to local and global climate change and are the primary driver of soil degradation (Coomes et al. 2001; Hasan et al. 2020; Zeller et al. 2017; Andreevich et al. 2020; Pan et al. 2023; Anees et al. 2024d). Traditional methods used phys
label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: Coomes et al. 2001; Hasan et al. 2020; Zeller et al. 2017; Andreevich et al. 2020; Pan et al. 2023; Anees et al. 2024d). Traditional methods used physical surveys for monitor - ing and classifying the LULC; now, these methods are lim- ited in their capacity to store collecting samples for large areas and often do not contain the whole variety of land use or land overs. Also, these methods are more costly and time-consuming (Alshari & Gawali, 2021; Zhang et al. 2024). Furthermore, motoring the LULC on a global scale makes it challenging to collect samples for all the LULC classes (Kindu et al. 2013). However, with the advance - ment in space and computer technologies in the last few decades, it has become possible to use Satellite imagery for the extraction of information about the earth’s sur - face and its environment, from small scale to large scale (Jansen and Antonio 2002; Rogan & Ch
label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: r the extraction of information about the earth’s sur - face and its environment, from small scale to large scale (Jansen and Antonio 2002; Rogan & Chen, 2004; Xi et al. 2022; Li et al. 2022; Badshah et al. 2017). Multisource remote sensing data have become increasingly available; multisource data includes satellite data like Landsat mul- tispectral imagery, hyperspectral airborne data, and radar data (Lassalle et al., 2023; Zhang 2010; Tu et al. 2024; Zhao et al. 2024; Yu et al. 2024). Remote sensing, com - bined with geographic information systems (GIS), has proven to be an effective tool for detecting and mapping LULC (Aboelnour and Engel 2018; Yan et al. 2023; Anees et al. 2022a). Remote sensing provides multi-spectral and multi-temporal data, which can turn into valuable informa- tion for understanding and motoring land use patterns and land cover processes, while GIS provides a fle
label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: l data, which can turn into valuable informa- tion for understanding and motoring land use patterns and land cover processes, while GIS provides a flexible envi- ronment for storing, analyzing, and displaying spatial data (Anees et al. 2022b; Pham et al., 2024; Ur Rehman et al., 2021; Yan et al. 2024; Khan et al. 2024). Satellite imagery is one the best solutions for monitoring land cover types by spectral classification or by biophysical characteristics of the land surface (Weng 2002; Weng, Hu, and Liu, 2009). In recent decades, remote sensing data combined with GIS has been extensively utilized to monitor spatial and temporal LULC patterns. For example, Shalaby and Tatei- shi (2007) demonstrated the effectiveness of multi-temporal Landsat TM/ETM + data in tracking land cover changes, while Qian et al. (2015) highlighted the capability of World- View-2 satellite imagery in accurately cl

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: TM/ETM + data in tracking land cover changes, while Qian et al. (2015) highlighted the capability of World- View-2 satellite imagery in accurately classifying land cover in urban areas. Similarly, Carlson and Arthur (2000) ana- lyzed temporal land cover changes using AVHRR and Land- sat TM data, showing how urbanization drives significant land cover transformations. These studies underscore the critical role of satellite imagery in capturing detailed LULC changes over time, which forms the basis for our compre- hensive evaluation of satellite sensors and classification algorithms. LULC classification methods for remote sensing data have been developed and tested in many past studies (Gašparović & Jogun, 2018; Gašparović, Zrinjski, and Gudelj 2019a; Pal & Mather, 2003). Recently, many researchers have devel- oped and tested different supervised methods for LULC clas- sification, such as
label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: lj 2019a; Pal & Mather, 2003). Recently, many researchers have devel- oped and tested different supervised methods for LULC clas- sification, such as maximum likelihood, minimum distance (Sohn et al., 2002; Nti Asamoah et al. 2018b), RF (Belgium & Drăgu, 2016; Rodriguez-Galiano et al. 2012a; Shao et al., 2024), SVM (Pal and Mather 2005; Vandewalle 1999), Deci- sion Tree Classification (Brodleyf 1997; Friedl and Brodley 1997) and KNN (Ding et al. 2024; Gašparović, Zrinjski, and Gudelj, 2019b). The selection of appropriate satellite sensors plays a cru- cial role in the accuracy of LULC classification. This study utilized three satellite sensors: GF-6, S2, and L9. GF-6, part of China’s High-resolution Earth Observation System (CHEOS), offers high spatial and spectral resolution across visible, infrared, and microwave bands. These features make it particularly suited for capturing detailed
label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: high spatial and spectral resolution across visible, infrared, and microwave bands. These features make it particularly suited for capturing detailed LULC classifi- cations, especially in complex environments with diverse land cover types (Muhammad et al. 2020). The GF series of satellites, including GF-6, are recognized for their ability to collect high-resolution, multi-spectral imagery with a fre- quent revisit schedule, enabling precise temporal and spatial monitoring of LULC changes (Han et al. 2024; Li 2022; Yu et al. 2020). Similarly, S2, has become a go-to source for LULC studies due to its free accessibility and global cover- age (Rehman, A. et al. 2024). It has multi-spectral capabili- ties, combined with its short revisit period, make it highly effective in detecting changes in land cover and vegetation health (Faruque et al. 2022; Jombo 2023; Thanh Noi & Kap- pas, 2017; Anees
label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: make it highly effective in detecting changes in land cover and vegetation health (Faruque et al. 2022; Jombo 2023; Thanh Noi & Kap- pas, 2017; Anees et al. 2024b). L9, launched in 2021, offers improvements over Landsat-8, with enhanced radiometric resolution and signal-to-noise ratio, particularly for darker features like forests and water bodies (Masek et al. 2020; Mehmood et al. 2024d). Its resolution is widely used in Earth Science Informatics (2025) 18:212 Page 3 of 22 212 large-scale LULC monitoring, contributing valuable data for global Earth observation programs (Estoque & Murayama, 2015; Hussain, Mehmood, Anees et al. 2024b). Including these three sensors in this study allows for a comprehen- sive assessment of how varying spatial resolutions influence LULC classification accuracy (Mehmood et al. 2024e). Choosing a suitable algorithm is critical for accurate LULC classification
label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: resolutions influence LULC classification accuracy (Mehmood et al. 2024e). Choosing a suitable algorithm is critical for accurate LULC classification (Xu et al. 2022). This study focuses on RF, SVM, and XGBoost, which offer computational efficiency and adaptability. While deep learning models such as Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) excel with high-resolution data, their resource-intensive requirements can limit accessibil- ity (Rodriguez-Galiano et al. 2012b). RF is robust and han- dles high-dimensional datasets effectively (Breiman 2001; Rodriguez-Galiano et al. 2012b; Shahzad et al. 2024). SVM manages complex, non-linear relationships, particularly in heterogeneous landscapes (Mehmood et al. 2024a; Moun- trakis et al., 2011; Khatami et al. 2016). XGBoost, known for its efficiency and accuracy, often surpasses both RF and SVM in handling large, comp
label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: un- trakis et al., 2011; Khatami et al. 2016). XGBoost, known for its efficiency and accuracy, often surpasses both RF and SVM in handling large, complex datasets (Georganos et al. 2018). While numerous studies have compared sensors and classification algorithms for LULC mapping, our research uniquely integrates high-resolution GF-6 data with S2 and L9 to assess their combined effectiveness in a heterogene- ous urban and semi-natural landscape. Furthermore, this study systematically evaluates the performance of advanced machine learning algorithms to determine the optimal sen- sor-algorithm pairing for accurate LULC classification. By focusing on Islamabad, Pakistan, a region characterized by rapid urbanization and diverse land cover, this study pro- vides valuable insights into the impact of spatial resolution and algorithmic selection on classification accuracy, filling a critical gap
label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: study pro- vides valuable insights into the impact of spatial resolution and algorithmic selection on classification accuracy, filling a critical gap in existing literature. The main objective of this study is to evaluate the accuracy of three classification algorithms RF, SVM, and XGBoost, using data from three satellite sensors: GF-6, S2, and L9. This study aims to inves- tigate the effect of spatial resolution on LULC classification accuracy and determine which algorithm performs best. The results will provide valuable insights into the optimal selection of classifiers and satellite data for accurate LULC classification. Study area Islamabad, the capital of Pakistan, is located between lati- tudes 33°28’12’’ N and longitude 72°48’36’’ E, covering an area of 906 km² with a population of approximately 2 million (Badshah et al., 2024). Situated adjacent to the urbanized region of Rawalpi
label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: covering an area of 906 km² with a population of approximately 2 million (Badshah et al., 2024). Situated adjacent to the urbanized region of Rawalpindi, the two cities form a signifi- cant urban and administrative hub in the country. The close geographical and ecological linkages between Islamabad and Rawalpindi make the region ideal for LULC studies, espe- cially as urban development in both cities is interconnected (Hussain, Mehmood, Yujun et al. 2024). The study area is characterized by diverse land cover types, including forested regions, vegetative land, imper - vious surfaces, barren lands, and water bodies, reflecting a mix of urban, natural, and semi-natural environments (Naeem et al. 2018; Aslam et al. 2022). Geologically, the region consists of tertiary sandstone, limestone, and allu - vial deposits, contributing to the varied landscape. Islama- bad’s climate is classified as
label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: gion consists of tertiary sandstone, limestone, and allu - vial deposits, contributing to the varied landscape. Islama- bad’s climate is classified as humid subtropical, featuring five distinct seasons: winter, spring, summer, monsoon, and autumn. The average air temperature ranges from 36 °C to 42 °C, with hot summers and mild winters (Butt et al. 2015). The area presents unique challenges for LULC classification due to its diverse land cover types, rapid urban expansion, and complex ecological characteristics. These factors make Islamabad a critical urban environmental monitoring and management case study. Figure 1 illustrates the geographic location of the study area. Materials and methods Field data collection Field data collection was conducted between November 10 and November 27, 2023. During the field visits, precise coordinates for various LULC classes were recorded using a handh

During the field visits, precise coordinates for various LULC classes were recorded using a handheld GPS receiver. The timing of the data collection coincided with the peak season for clear differentiation of vegetation, water bodies, and built structures, allowing for enhanced accuracy in land cover classification. The LULC classes considered in this study were forest land, agricultural land, built-up areas, barren land, and water bodies, ensuring a comprehensive representation of the diverse land cover types in the region (refer to Table 2 for descriptions of the LULC types in Table 1). A total of 910 samples were collected in the field, and these samples were subsequently divided into training and testing datasets using a 70:30 split ratio. This method fol- lows the approach of previous studies (e.g., Adelabu and Dube (2015); Jombo
label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

This method fol- lows the approach of previous studies (e.g., Adelabu and Dube (2015); Jombo et al. (2020); Odindi, Bangamwabo, and Mutanga (2015), which have shown that this split achieves high classification accuracy with minimal variabil- ity. Specifically, 710 samples were allocated for training the machine learning models, while 279 samples were reserved for testing the model’s performance (see Table 2 for sample distribution). The training samples served as input data for the classifi- cation algorithms, enabling the accurate mapping of LULC in the study area. In contrast, the testing samples were used Earth Science Informatics (2025) 18:212 212 Page 4 of 22 for validation and accuracy assessment. Preparation of training samples Preparing training samples is a crucial step in LULC clas- sification, as it establishes the foundation for model accu- racy and generalization (Jozdani, S. E. 2019; Chen et al. 2023; Xie et al. 2023; Sun et al. 2023; Liu et al. 2024). This study generated training samples by delineating polygons on Google Satellite imagery. These polygons represented distinct land use categories across the study area. The classi- fication process benefited from this method, enabling spatial representation of homogeneous land cover types (Jiang et al. 2024; Zhou et al. 2022a, b, c;
label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: tion process benefited from this method, enabling spatial representation of homogeneous land cover types (Jiang et al. 2024; Zhou et al. 2022a, b, c; Chen et al. 2024). A total of 910 training polygons were created, corresponding to five key land use categories: 245 polygons for forest land (FL), 160 for agricultural land (AG), 130 for built-up areas (BU), 80 for barren land (BL), and 95 for water bodies (WB). The sizes of these polygons varied depending on the extent and homogeneity of the land cover types, ranging from 10 to 150 square meters. Larger polygons were delineated for homoge- neous classes, such as agricultural fields, while smaller poly- gons were created for fragmented or heterogeneous areas, such as built-up regions. Field-collected ground validation points (GVPs) were also used to train further and evalu- ate the models. Of the GVPs collected during field surveys using G
label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: ollected ground validation points (GVPs) were also used to train further and evalu- ate the models. Of the GVPs collected during field surveys using GPS precision, 70% were allocated for training, and these were cross-referenced with high-resolution satellite imagery to ensure alignment with the generated polygons and other reference data. The two complementary training datasets ensured the models were robust and representative of the diversity of land cover types across the study area. To minimize the inclusion of mixed-class pixels, only central pixels within each polygon were prioritized for training, and the polygons were manually validated to ensure their con- sistency with high-resolution imagery. The training samples served as the foundation for the classification models devel- oped in this study. The data from L9, S2, and GF-6 were pro- cessed using R software, with the terra and

The data from L9, S2, and GF-6 were pro- cessed using R software, with the terra and rgdal packages facilitating spatial data analysis and processing (Bivand and Hauke 2013). This systematic approach to generating train- ing samples allowed for reliable and consistent classification across different sensors, capturing the diversity of land cover types within the study area. Machine learning algorithms for LULC classification This research employed RF, XGBoost, and SVM algorithms to classify LULC from high-resolution satellite imagery, including L9, S2, and GF-6 data. RF, a robust ensemble learning algorithm, is widely used due to its ability to han- dle high-dimensional and noisy datasets. It creates multi - ple decision trees from random subsets of the training data, improving classification accuracy through majority voti

It creates multi - ple decision trees from random subsets of the training data, improving classification accuracy through majority voting. Hyperparameters such as the number of trees (ntree) and the number of predictors considered at each split (mtry) were optimized to enhance classification accuracy. The RF algorithm operates through an ensemble of decision trees, where each tree ℎ(𝑥, Θ𝑘) contributes a vote for classifying a data point x. Here, Θk represents independently distributed random vectors that define the trees (Anees et al. 2024a; Luo et al. 2024). The ensemble was supplied for our study by adjusting the number of trees (ntree) and the number of predictors considered at each split (mtry). The ntree parameter was varied from 500 to 10,000 in increments of 1,000, and mtry was adjusted between 1 and 10 to optimize the model for maximum classification accuracy. The

The application of RF in our study highlights its effectiveness in interpreting the com- plex dynamics of LULC, leveraging its ensemble approach to produce reliable and understandable results (Anees et al. 2024c; Luo et al. 2024). XGBoost is an efficient gradient-boosting algorithm that was utilized for its ability to handle complex datasets and prevent overfitting. Its regularized boosting technique helps improve generalization, and its performance was optimized through tuning parameters such as learning rate and tree depth. XGBoost’s computational efficiency and ability to capture non-linear relationships make it particularly suitable for high-resolution LULC datasets. Feature importance analysis for LULC classification models To evaluate the contribution of different features to LULC classification, feature importance was assessed using meth- ods specific to each machine learning algorithm. For tree- based models, such as RF and XGBoost, the Gini index was used to measure the decrease in node impurity, providing a quantitative assessment of feature significance. For the SVM algorithm, feature importance was inferred based on the magnitude of model coefficients. This analy

For the SVM algorithm, feature importance was inferred based on the magnitude of model coefficients. This analysis was per- formed across three satellite sensors: L9, S2, and GF-6. For RF and XGBoost models, the Gini impurity decrease was computed using the Ranger package in R, while SVM fea- ture importance was derived from coefficient-based meth- ods. This comprehensive approach allowed for identifying the most critical predictors in the classification models, offering insights into the influence of each feature on clas - sification accuracy. Results and discussion Algorithm performance based on accuracy assessment in land use and land cover classification This study evaluated the performance of three machine learning algorithms (RF, XGBoost, and SVM) (Fig. 3) applied to data from three remote sensing sensors: L9, S2, and GF-6. The goal was to assess their effectiveness in classifying five distinct LULC types. Using the Random Forest algorithm with L9, an overall accuracy of 91.27% and a kappa coefficient of 0.8908 were achieved, indicat - ing strong classification capability (Table 4). Precision and recall were exceptionally high in mixed land cover classes, showing RF’s robustness in handling varied feat
label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

text: From the paper "Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land cover classification in Islamabad, Pakistan" (2025) by Arif Ur Rahman: cation capability (Table 4). Precision and recall were exceptionally high in mixed land cover classes, showing RF’s robustness in handling varied features. This reflects RF’s ability to manage high-dimensional data effec- tively, particularly in heterogeneous areas like the study region, where mixed land cover classes are prevalent. How- ever, the slight drop in performance for urban classifica - tions highlights RF’s limitations in resolving subtle spectral differences in classes dominated by impervious surfaces (Swetanisha et al. 2022; Mehmood et al. 2024b). However, the performance of S2 declined, with accuracy at 85.22% and a kappa of 0.8152, possibly due to the sensor’s spectral characteristics. The lower resolution of Sentinel-2 com- pared to GF-6 might have contributed to misclassification in smaller and fragmented features, especially in urban and barren classes. Additionally, th
label: Paper: Comparative analysis of sensors and classification algorithms for land (2025)

Additionally, the less distinct spectral sepa- rability of certain LULC classes may have impacted RF’s ability to discriminate between them effectively (Matyukira et al. 2023; Keshtkar et al. (2019). On the other hand, RF paired with GF-6 yielded improved performance, with an accuracy of 92.65% and a kappa of 0.9081, suggesting better synergy between GF-6’s data quality and RF in capturing detailed class features. XGBoost consistently delivered strong results across all sensors (Table 5). With L9, XGBoost achieved 91.20% accuracy and a kappa of 0.89, excelling in urban and vegeta- tion classifications due to its capacity to handle non-linear relationships (Rahman et al. 2021; Georganos et al. 2018).
